title: On the Genealogy of Morality - Debt, Guilt, and God
author: Friedrich Nietzsche
safe_use_note: Use to explain moralized debt and guilt, not to trivialize real harm or accountability.
misreading_risk: Can be misread as reducing all morality to economics.
raw_text:
TITLE: Debt, Guilt, and God
WORK: On the Genealogy of Morality
SECTION: Second Essay, selected excerpt
AUTHOR: Friedrich Nietzsche
THEMES: debt, guilt, punishment, God, bad conscience
SOURCE_TYPE: text

According to the teaching of history, the consciousness of owing debts to the deity by no means came to an end with the decay of the clan organisation of society. Just as mankind has inherited the ideas of good and bad from the race-nobility, so with the heritage of the racial and tribal gods it has also inherited the incubus of debts as yet unpaid and the desire to discharge them.

The feeling of owing a debt to the deity has grown continuously for several centuries, always in the same proportion in which the idea of God and the consciousness of God have grown and become exalted among mankind. The appearance of the Christian god, as the record god up to this time, has for that very reason brought equally into the world the record amount of guilt consciousness.

It is with the moralisation of the ideas "ought" and "duty," and with their being pushed back into the bad conscience, that comes the first actual attempt to reverse the direction of development. At this juncture the ideas "guilt" and "duty" have to turn backwards—turn backwards against whom? Primarily against the ower, in whom the bad conscience now establishes itself, eats, extends, and grows like a polypus.

With the impossibility of paying the debt, there becomes conceived the idea of the impossibility of paying the penalty, the thought of its inexpiability—eternal punishment. Finally it turns against the creditor, whether found in the origin of the human race, in Nature, or in existence generally.

Suddenly we stand before that paradoxical and awful expedient, through which a tortured humanity has found a temporary alleviation, that stroke of genius called Christianity: God personally immolating himself for the debt of man, God paying himself personally out of a pound of his own flesh, God as the one being who can deliver man from what man had become unable to deliver himself—the creditor playing scapegoat for his debtor, from love.

Owing something to God becomes the instrument of torture. Man apprehends in God the most extreme antitheses that he can find to his own ineradicable animal instincts, and gives a new interpretation to these instincts as being against what he owes to God. He poisons the fundamental basis of existence with guilt and punishment.
